definition: A/R Days, or Accounts Receivable Days, measures how long it takes a healthcare organization to collect payment after services are billed.
example: A hospital may review A/R Days to understand whether claims are getting paid quickly or sitting unpaid with Medicare, Medicaid, commercial payers, or patients.
whyItMatters: High A/R Days can signal delayed cash, payer follow-up issues, billing delays, authorization problems, or denials that are not being resolved quickly.

definition: Clean Claim Rate measures the percentage of claims submitted correctly the first time without needing correction, rework, or additional information.
example: If a claim is submitted with the correct patient information, insurance details, codes, modifiers, and documentation, it is more likely to be accepted as a clean claim.
whyItMatters: A higher clean claim rate usually means fewer delays, fewer denials, faster reimbursement, and stronger revenue cycle performance.

definition: Denial Rate is the percentage of claims denied by payers after submission.
example: A payer may deny a claim because prior authorization was missing, eligibility was not verified, documentation was incomplete, or coding was incorrect.
whyItMatters: Denials delay cash and create rework. Consultants often analyze denials by payer, reason code, department, provider, or process step.

definition: DNFB stands for Discharged Not Final Billed. It refers to accounts for patients who have been discharged but whose final bill has not yet been sent.
example: A patient leaves the hospital, but the account is waiting for coding, documentation, charge review, or billing edits before the claim can be submitted.
whyItMatters: High DNFB can delay revenue and may indicate issues in coding, documentation, charge capture, or billing workflows.

definition: Charge capture is the process of recording the services, procedures, supplies, medications, or tests provided to a patient so they can be billed.
example: If a patient receives an imaging test or medication, that service needs to be captured correctly so the hospital can bill for it.
whyItMatters: Missed or inaccurate charge capture can create revenue leakage, compliance risk, billing errors, and inaccurate financial reporting.

definition: Payer mix describes the proportion of patients or revenue coming from different payer types, such as Medicare, Medicaid, commercial insurance, self-pay, or managed care.
example: A hospital with a large Medicaid population may face different reimbursement dynamics than one with a larger commercial insurance population.
whyItMatters: Payer mix affects reimbursement, margins, financial planning, access strategy, and how health systems evaluate service-line performance.

definition: Value-based care is a healthcare payment and delivery model that rewards quality, outcomes, efficiency, and patient experience rather than only volume of services.
example: A provider organization may be rewarded for reducing readmissions, improving preventive care, or managing chronic conditions more effectively.
whyItMatters: Value-based care changes how health systems think about quality, cost, patient outcomes, care coordination, and performance improvement.

definition: HCAHPS is a standardized survey that measures patients’ perspectives on hospital care and patient experience.
example: Hospitals may review HCAHPS scores related to communication with nurses, communication with doctors, discharge information, and overall hospital rating.
whyItMatters: HCAHPS affects public reporting, patient experience strategy, quality improvement, and in some cases reimbursement-related programs.

definition: Readmission Rate measures how often patients return to the hospital within a certain period after discharge, commonly within 30 days.
example: A hospital may monitor patients readmitted after heart failure, pneumonia, or surgery to identify gaps in discharge planning or follow-up care.
whyItMatters: High readmission rates can indicate issues with care transitions, discharge education, outpatient follow-up, medication management, or quality of care.

definition: A hospital-acquired condition is a complication or adverse condition that develops during a hospital stay and may have been preventable.
example: Examples may include certain infections, pressure injuries, patient falls with injury, or other preventable safety events.
whyItMatters: These conditions affect patient safety, quality scores, regulatory performance, reimbursement, and public trust.

definition: The item master is the central list of supplies, products, equipment, and materials used by an organization, including item descriptions, units of measure, pricing, and vendor information.
example: A hospital’s item master may include gloves, syringes, implants, surgical supplies, office supplies, and other materials used across departments.
whyItMatters: A messy item master can cause ordering errors, duplicate items, pricing issues, reporting problems, and supply chain inefficiency.

definition: PAR level is the target quantity of an item that should be kept on hand in a supply location.
example: If a supply room has a PAR level of 20 boxes of gloves, staff replenish the location back to 20 when inventory drops below the target.
whyItMatters: Incorrect PAR levels can lead to stockouts, overstocking, wasted supplies, clinical frustration, and unnecessary working capital.

definition: A stockout occurs when a needed item is unavailable when and where it is required.
example: A nursing unit may experience a stockout if a commonly used supply is not available in the supply room during patient care.
whyItMatters: Stockouts can delay care, create workarounds, increase emergency ordering, frustrate staff, and indicate supply chain process issues.

definition: A preference card lists the supplies, instruments, equipment, and setup preferences needed for a specific procedure or provider.
example: An operating room team may use a surgeon preference card to prepare the correct supplies for a knee replacement procedure.
whyItMatters: Inaccurate preference cards can lead to waste, missing supplies, case delays, unnecessary picking, and higher procedural costs.

definition: Contract compliance measures whether purchases are being made according to negotiated supplier contracts, pricing, and terms.
example: A hospital may have a contract price for a supply item, but if the item is purchased outside the contract, the organization may pay more than expected.
whyItMatters: Poor contract compliance can create avoidable cost, reduce savings capture, and weaken procurement performance.

definition: CDI stands for Clinical Documentation Integrity. It focuses on improving the accuracy, completeness, and clarity of clinical documentation.
example: A CDI specialist may query a provider to clarify documentation so the medical record accurately reflects the patient’s severity of illness and care provided.
whyItMatters: CDI affects coding accuracy, reimbursement, quality reporting, compliance, provider communication, and patient data integrity.

definition: An EHR, or Electronic Health Record, is the digital system used to document, manage, and access patient clinical information.
example: Clinicians may use an EHR to document encounters, place orders, review lab results, manage medications, and coordinate patient care.
whyItMatters: EHR workflow design affects clinician efficiency, documentation quality, patient safety, reporting, and care coordination.

definition: A clinical workflow is the sequence of steps clinicians and staff follow to deliver care, document information, and coordinate patient activities.
example: A medication administration workflow may include order entry, pharmacist verification, nurse review, barcode scanning, administration, and documentation.
whyItMatters: Poor workflows can create delays, duplicate work, documentation burden, safety risks, and clinician frustration.

definition: Provider productivity measures the volume or value of clinical work performed by a physician or advanced practice provider.
example: A medical group may review visits, wRVUs, panel size, access, or procedural volume to understand provider productivity.
whyItMatters: Productivity affects access, compensation models, revenue, staffing, scheduling, and physician enterprise performance.

definition: wRVU stands for Work Relative Value Unit. It is used to measure the relative work effort associated with clinical services.
example: A physician compensation plan may use wRVUs to help measure productivity and determine incentive compensation.
whyItMatters: wRVUs influence provider productivity analysis, compensation design, access planning, and practice financial performance.

definition: Patient access refers to how easily patients can schedule, reach, and receive care from providers or services.
example: A medical group may study appointment availability, call center performance, referral management, and scheduling templates to improve patient access.
whyItMatters: Poor access can reduce patient satisfaction, delay care, lower provider utilization, and affect revenue.

definition: Vacancy Rate measures the percentage of approved positions that are currently unfilled.
example: A hospital may monitor nursing vacancy rates by unit, shift, role, or location to understand staffing gaps.
whyItMatters: High vacancy rates can increase overtime, agency labor use, burnout, access constraints, and operational risk.

definition: Overtime Rate measures how much labor is being worked beyond standard scheduled hours.
example: A department may use overtime when staffing levels are low, patient volume is high, or schedules are not aligned with demand.
whyItMatters: High overtime can increase labor cost, signal staffing shortages, contribute to burnout, and reveal scheduling or productivity issues.

definition: FTE stands for Full-Time Equivalent. It is a way to measure staffing capacity based on full-time work hours.
example: Two employees working half-time may equal one FTE for workforce planning and budget purposes.
whyItMatters: FTE analysis supports budgeting, productivity measurement, staffing models, scheduling, and labor cost management.

definition: A legacy system is an older technology platform that an organization still uses, often because it supports critical business processes.
example: A hospital acquired by a health system may still use Lawson while the parent organization uses Workday.
whyItMatters: Legacy systems create challenges during ERP implementations because data, workflows, customizations, and integrations must be evaluated and migrated carefully.

definition: Data conversion is the process of transforming data from one system’s format into the structure required by another system.
example: During a Lawson-to-Workday transition, employee, supplier, item, and financial data may need to be converted into Workday-compatible formats.
whyItMatters: Poor data conversion can cause go-live defects, reporting issues, payment errors, process disruption, and user mistrust.

definition: Data mapping defines how fields in one system correspond to fields in another system.
example: A legacy job code may need to map to a new Workday job profile, supervisory organization, compensation grade, or worker type.
whyItMatters: Accurate mapping helps ensure data moves correctly and supports clean reporting, security, workflow, and downstream integrations.

definition: UAT stands for User Acceptance Testing. It is the phase where end users test whether a system supports real business processes before go-live.
example: Hospital HR, finance, supply chain, or clinical operations users may test hiring, purchasing, invoice processing, or reporting workflows before a Workday go-live.
whyItMatters: UAT helps catch process, configuration, data, security, and training issues before the system is used in production.

definition: Cutover is the planned transition from an old system or process to a new system or process.
example: During an ERP implementation, cutover may include final data loads, system freeze periods, validation steps, user access setup, and go-live readiness checks.
whyItMatters: A poor cutover can disrupt payroll, purchasing, billing, reporting, operations, and user confidence during go-live.
